---
title: "Colleges"
author: "Henry Yang"
format: html
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| message: false
library(tidyverse)
library(primer.data)
```

```{r}
colleges |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = tuition, y = grad_rate)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Graduation Rate vs. Tuition",
    subtitle = "Higher tuition is associated with higher graduation rates",
    x = "Tuition (USD)",
    y = "Graduation Rate (%)",
    caption = "Source: colleges dataset from primer.data"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#| cache: true
x <- colleges |> 
  select(tuition, grad_rate, selectivity) |> 
  filter(tuition > 2) |> 
  drop_na() |> 
  mutate(selectivity = factor(
    selectivity, 
    levels = c("Elite", "Highly Selective", "Moderately Selective", "Lowly Selective", "Non-selective")
  ))
```


## Summary
U.S. College graduation rates vary depending on various factors: one impactful factor is tuition. We used data from the Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System (IPEDS) from 2013 to estimate the difference in expected graduation rates between colleges with tuition of $20,000 and colleges with tuition of $30,000.
